Q3 2010/11 TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD Earnings Conference Call - Final https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/q3-2010-11-tata-consultancy-services-ltd-earnings/docview/848427836/se-2?accountid=27562 Abstract: None available. Full text: OPERATOR: Ladies and gentlemen, good day, and welcome to the TCS earnings conference call. Please note that for the duration of this presentation, all participant lines will be in the listen-only mode and this conference is being recorded. After the presentation there will be an opportunity for you to ask questions. (Operator Instructions). At this time I would like to turn the conference over to Mr. Kedar Shirali, Director of Investor Relations at TCS. Thank you and over to you, Mr. Shirali. KEDAR SHIRALI, HEAD OF IR, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Thanks, Michelle. Good evening and welcome everyone. I wish you all a very happy new year. We are here to discuss TCS's financial results for the third quarter of fiscal year 2011 that ended December 31, 2010. This call is being webcast through our website and an archive including the transcript will be available on the site for the duration of this quarter. The financial statements, results presentation and press releases are also available on our website. Our leadership team is present on this call to discuss our results. We have with us today, Mr. N. Chandrasekaran, Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director. N. CHANDRASEKARAN, CEO, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hello, good morning, good afternoon, and good evening, everyone. KEDAR SHIRALI: Mr. S. Mahalingam, Chief Financial Officer and Executive Director. S. MAHALINGAM, CFO, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hello, everyone. KEDAR SHIRALI: Mr. Phiroz Vandrevala, Head of Global Corporate Affairs and Executive Director. PHIROZ VANDREVALA, HEAD, GLOBAL CORPORATE, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hi, everyone. KEDAR SHIRALI: And Mr. Ajoy Mukherjee, Head of Global Human Resources. AJOY MUKHERJEE, HEAD, GLOBAL HUMAN RESOURCES, TATA CONSULTANCY SERVICES LTD: Hi, everyone. KEDAR SHIRALI: Chandra and Mahal will give us a brief overview of the Company's performance followed by the q-and-a session. As you are aware we do not provide specific revenue and earnings guidance. Anything said on this call which reflects our outlook for the future always can be construed as a forward-looking statement, must be reviewed

in conjunction with the risks that the Company faces. We have outlined these risks in the second slide of the results presentation that was emailed to you earlier this evening and which is also available on our website. With that I'd like to turn the call over to Chandra to begin the proceedings. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thank you, Kedar. Let me take a few minutes to give you some highlights and the outlook. We have been able to deliver another stellar quarter. The revenues have gone up 4.1% on rupee terms and 7% on dollar terms. If you only look at the international business it has gone up by 7.8% and volumes are up nicely at 5.7%. We have been able to deliver an all-around solid performance in the margins. The gross margin has gone up by 26 basis points, the operating level 7 basis points, and at the net margin 144 basis points. We have seen quite (inaudible) growth in this quarter at 118 basis points. In terms of the markets, the major markets have led the growth. US, UK, and Continental Europe and Asia Pacific have done exceedingly well. India and Latin America have shown decline, primarily because the mix we have in these markets still are not fully annuity based, they have a lot of discretionary portion as well, and in the December quarter the projects lined up were wind-downs have happened and then we have not seen growth in these two markets. In terms of the industries, all the industries have shown growth, even telecom which has shown a decline. If you exclude the India business in telecom it has shown growth. So, all the industries have delivered good growth. If we look at the industries which are smaller, they have shown double-digit growth, industries like hi-tech, media, travel and hospitality, and the utility industries have done a high level of growth. In terms of services we have seen excellent growth in our asset leverage business, in infrastructure business, BPO business and assurance-related business. The deal closures have been solid and they have come in different sizes

and they have also across verticals and across markets. Overall, what we see in the environment is while the macro continues to be very dynamic both from an efficiency point of view as well as from a transformation or a growth point of view customers are continuing to spend. And their behavior is different in different markets and the priorities are different with different customers. But, across the board we are seeing good opportunities and we think the outlook is good and we have also gone ahead with an excellent hiring this quarter, over 20,000 people gross addition, over 12,000 people net addition, and the original target of [50,000] people has already been exceeded this quarter. We think we will add another about 12,000 to 15,000 people in Q4. From a spend point of view, most customers, the situation will be either at their current spend level or they will be increasing their spending. I do expect an uptake in their discretionary spend, going by what we see currently. Those are my initial comments and I'll now hand over to Mahal for his detailed comments. Then we'll get on to questions. S. MAHALINGAM: Okay, thanks, Chandra, and Chandra has already walked you through some of the numbers. So, we had a global (inaudible) rate volume growth of 5.7% accompanied by a constant currency pricing -- constant pricing improvement of 1.2%. The strengthening rupee led to an exchange impact of minus 1.7% and increased offshore leverage led to an impact of minus 1.1% in revenue. Adding these up you get a 4.1% INR sequential growth. With growth momentum picking up, expenses associated with revenue generation such as travel and communication continued to go up as expected. We also continued to invest in building capacity to hire -- handle the large numbers. In addition, we have to contend with the strengthening rupee, the realized rate in INR against dollar and GBP were 2.7% and 1.7% less this quarter over the Q2 rates. Despite such formidable headwinds we were still able to expand the EBIT margin by 7 bps to 28.08% through improved

pricing, offshore shift, and continued containment of costs in other areas which are not growth linked. The impact of different levers of operating margin is as follows. Currency, minus 112 basis points; impact of offshore shift, plus 23 basis points; rate of productivity and other SG&A efficiencies, 90 basis points; total EBIT margin 28.08%. Other income was higher this quarter due to a net foreign exchange gain of INR521m against a loss last quarter and higher interest income from invested cash. Our effective tax rate was 18.6% giving us a net income of INR23.302b, that's a net margin of 24.12%, a 144 basis points expansion q-on-q. Lastly, our DSO for the quarter was 80 days. Invested funds stood at INR85.7b. So, in conclusion, the continued growth momentum we saw in Q3 is evidence of a broadly diversified growth engine that is running smoothly, consistently delivering us strong, high quality volumes quarter after quarter. Likewise our ability to hold the EBIT margin at what we think is ideal level for TCS reflects the sustainability of our cost at a structural level. With this we can open up for questions. OPERATOR: Thank you very much. (Operator Instructions). Our first question is from the line of Joseph Foresi of Janney Montgomery Scott. Please go ahead. JOSEPH FORESI, ANALYST, JANNEY MONTGOMERY SCOTT: Hi. My first question here is one of your competitors had talked about a lack of a budget flush at the end of the year, but the demand trends being strong heading into 2011. Did you experience something similar to that? Maybe you could just talk a little bit about that commentary towards the end of this year. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think if you look at the Q3 performances, basically we have continued the momentum and I wouldn't say that there is any specific budget flush and that there is no indication of a one-off flush-related growth in any specific account or in the industry or in the market. So, I think the momentum has been good s I said whether it's a market or a customer where there is an economic

uncertainty or not. The initiatives for using technology, both from an efficiency point of view or from a growth point of view, is something that we are witnessing and that is gaining opportunities and we are able to capture the opportunity because the business model is very attractive. JOSEPH FORESI: Maybe you could just -- I think the Americas slowed a little bit, maybe application development work slowed a little bit, do you expect that to reaccelerate heading into next year once budgets are set or how should we think about the trends in those two businesses? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See, currently I don't expect a drag in any of those areas, whether it is Americas or the application development. I think both are in line and they are doing well and, as I said, the overall momentum is good. JOSEPH FORESI: And then just finally, we saw a pricing increase, is that a trend that you're expecting to continue heading into next year? Should we -- how quickly are people accepting price increases and do you expect that to continue through '011? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, I would be cautious on the pricing increase because the macro environment is still a bit dynamic so the pricing uptake I wouldn't take a call. Definitely it will be stable with an upward bias is the commentary I would give, but I wouldn't put a target on pricing increase at this point. JOSEPH FORESI: Okay, thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Foresi. Our next question is from the line of Vihang Naik of MF Global. Please go ahead. VIHANG NAIK, ANALYST, MF GLOBAL: Yes, hi, congrats on a very good quarter. IMS has basically contributed on an average about 20% to your incremental revenues over the past four quarters, despite just about 10% revenue contribution. I'm just curious about what are you exactly doing right in this vertical and doing better than your competitors? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Hopefully, we are doing many things right in many verticals and many services, not only in IMS, and this is an [in] business, it's been growing rapidly. I

think we have a very strong capability and offerings and more importantly proof points. We created this for the international market only in late 2003/2004. It has already crossed an annual [toll-ment] basis of INR700m range and we have currently this year robust opportunities, we were able to take on larger contracts and execute on them. I think it's a huge opportunity in terms of the space because of the size of the infrastructure contracts involved. So, I expect the growth momentum in infrastructure services to continue in the coming quarters. And also, frankly, we have had some excellent deal wins which have not yet resulted in revenues; they will be coming in the next fiscal. VIHANG NAIK: Okay, and just secondly, can you give us some outlook about some things that you are seeing in BFSI, especially for the regulatory and compliance areas. Do you really expect some kind of a huge spending in those areas and also how mainly spend is shaping up? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think in the BFSI in some of the major customers where we were involved in the integration projects, those integration projects have been completed. But, then we have been able to sustain the growth due to either compliance-related work or work related to new technologies which are resulting in higher [analytics] and customer insight, cross selling, upselling, those kind of initiatives, or re-platform initiatives where there are some core systems that are being replaced. So, we are seeing good momentum. In fact, if you look at our asset leverage business, the ForEx business, the BaNCS business has delivered a 23% growth this quarter on a sequential basis. So, I think the momentum in BFSI is good. The outlook, from all I can see, is very good. VIHANG NAIK: Yes, thanks. That's it from my side. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Naik. Our next question is from the line of Sandeep Shah of RBS. Please go ahead. SANDEEP SHAH, ANALYST, RBS: Hello. Just in terms of the ADM, if you look at the percentage contribution, early around six to seven quarters back, it was

higher than today's contribution by almost 400, 200 basis points. So, any trend one should read regarding this? So, the other services have increased significantly. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Sandeep, when the ADM is the first vertical with almost 100% of our revenues and we keep introducing new verticals, as a percentage of revenue, it will continue to keep going down. What is important is we are continuing to grow in area, in some quarters it is at -- the sequential growth rate is high, in some quarters it is lower single digits. But, the point is that ADM continues to grow. And ADM, we also see a lot of discretionary spend so as the discretionary spend picks up transformation projects will also come in ADM. So, I don't see any negatives should be taken from ADM growth at all. SANDEEP SHAH: Okay. And just as the deal renewals, which come up, you all commented on which service lines where the largest renewals are happening? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think it is the ADM is happening. The [trust security] is happening, BPO is happening, all those areas. SANDEEP SHAH: Okay, thanks. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Shah. Our next question is from the line of [Bhavin Shah] of Equirus. Please go ahead. BHAVIN SHAH, ANALYST, EQUIRUS: Yes, thanks. An excellent third quarter, sir. My first question is related to your BaNCS product. Any initiatives around the North American geography for that product that you can talk about? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, the BaNCS is an integrated platform with a lot of capability and we have installations for BaNCS in North America already, but not in the core banking area, but in many of the other areas like securities, custody and so on. And we continue to pursue opportunities for all the segments of banks whether it is core banking, whether it is payments, securities, treasury, or cash management, or custody, or market infrastructure. I think there are a number of opportunities and we expect to grow in North American banks. BHAVIN SHAH: And do you feel that some of the new recent regulatory changes creates

better opportunities, or makes it more easier for you to grab that opportunity than before, or it's any such ... N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See, I think that BaNCS is now a very mature product which is in the cellular technology. You may have seen during the quarter we announced a go-live offer, a very large core banking installation which went very successfully. BaNCS is rated as the leader by many analysts' reports and many others' reports as well this year. So, we think that whether it is a regulatory compliance related or transformation related because some of the institutions are looking to replace the core or the heart of the operation because they want to get more customer insight or integrated platform, etc., BaNCS holds an excellent opportunity and also we are seeing a lot of traction for the insurance side in BaNCS. There also we have won a few deals and we are competing in a few more deals. BHAVIN SHAH: Okay. And looking at the next year, do you feel that both you and industry will be more prepared for growth and as a result maybe more campus hiring and also maybe less of lateral movements and hence lower wage inflation, is that a reasonable conclusion? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think we are hiring a lot already; we have not only hired (inaudible) people this quarter and for next year we already made more than 23,000 offers. Ajoy has got the exact number and so our recruitment pipeline is pretty strong. And I really can't comment on the wage issue yet, I think we are still working on all the details on what is going to be the increments. As we do well we will also look at what kind of a (inaudible) we have to make. BHAVIN SHAH: Just a last question from me. I think for the Indian market in semi-segment you have some pilots going out in terms of cloud offering. Can you talk a little bit about that and do you see that as something that's going to be very meaningful in the future or it's

too early to say? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: We will not do it if it's not meaningful. We think it's certainly very important and it's going to be meaningful in size and scale. That's where we are foreseeing that and we are very happy with all the pilots. We have crossed 100 customers. We will do a formal launch in Q4. BHAVIN SHAH: Okay. Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Shah. Our next question is from the line of Srivathsan Ramachandran of Spark Capital. Please go ahead. SRIVATHSAN RAMACHANDRAN, ANALYST, SPARK CAPITAL: Hi, I just wanted to get some in particular, the pricing move was it more driven by a mix change in terms of your business or was it more on a like-for-like bases? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: What is your question? UNIDENTIFIED COMPANY REPRESENTATIVE: He is asking whether the rise is due to mix change in the portfolio, higher value, or is it like-for-like pricing improvement, N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think generally it's a like-for-like improvement. SRIVATHSAN RAMACHANDRAN: That's great. And is it more driven by new deals or renewals or is it --- I just want to know what kind of deals (inaudible) price improvements? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think it is a combination, some of the renewals and some of the new contracts that we have signed are coming up in scale. Because you know pricing takes a while and the new contracts on this scale also you see the improvement. So a combination of both. SRIVATHSAN RAMACHANDRAN: And on your BPO business you had a (inaudible - technical difficulty) Q4 and a somewhat [softer] Q3. Just wanted to know because as a year BPO has been slightly been slower than IT Services, how do you see the BPO outlook for calendar year '11 or maybe a cycle at this point of time given that BPO tends to lag traditional application development. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, I think with BPO we look at two parts. O the vertical side BPO, we are seeing traction in retail, we are seeing traction in pharma and also travel, apart from banking and BFSI. On the platform

side all the platforms that we have created, they have all gone live, and we need to scale them. We have started getting successes. They are contributing to revenue, albeit very small, and we expect the ramp ups to start coming in in the next fiscal year. So, I hope we do well in BPO. SRIVATHSAN RAMACHANDRAN: Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Ramachandran. Our next question is from the line of Kuldeep Kaul of Motilal Oswal Securities Limited. Please go ahead. KULDEEP KAUL, ANALYST, MOTILAL OSWAL SECURITIES LTD: Hello. Thanks for taking my question. My question is basically if I look at your data over the last two years when I look at specific vertical and service line exposure, manufacturing plus hi-tech used to be around 17% plus of revenues a couple of years ago, and now it's down to around 12% of your revenues. At the same time package implementation and consulting used to be 23% of revenues and it's now down to 17%. So, as far as package implementation and consulting is concerned, TCS used to be a bigger player than Infosys two to three years ago, whereas today Infosys is a bigger player. So, I just want to understand the dynamics. Whether it is any loss of competitive positioning within PI that is leading to the decline of manufacturing as a proportion of the mix or is it specific client exposure within manufacturing hi-tech which is consequently leading to a decline in package implementation consulting mix? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No, actually if you look at manufacturing, we had during the long term we had a huge hit, also our manufacturing revenue we had a significant exposure to the auto sector when the whole industry tanked. At that time we came down very sharply on manufacturing and now all I can say is that the last three quarters we are continuing to maintain the manufacturing share of the overall revenues whereas if you take hi-tech it is continuing to get better every quarter and we've had excellent deal wins as well. So, I think from here it will

grow but I just said we had a very sharp decline in manufacturing during the crisis. So, I would not read anything more than that. Plus we were involved in many large-scale transformation projects, all of those got halted, at that time and if you go back to the March, I mean the December 2007 then March 2008 period and then the subsequent quarters we continuously came down every quarter on manufacturing. So, I think we are regaining that growth at this point so I wouldn't complain. KULDEEP KAUL: Okay. So, you would expect the growth in each of these, the manufacturing hi-tech vertical as well as package implementation consulting to at least be similar as a proportion of the mix, or ideally be growing as a proportion of the mix from here on? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, that's what I'm looking forward to. KULDEEP KAUL: Okay. Thanks for that. And second question I have is when I look at your cash flow statement for the nine-month period even though the profit after tax has increased from INR49b to INR63b, the cash flow from operating activities is actually down for nine-months 2009 to nine-months 2010. So, from INR57b to around INR53b, can you just give some color around what's going on there? S. MAHALINGAM: This is a question that has been answered even in the last quarter when we looked at the six-month figures. See, basically, what has happened is there are a few things. One is the [extra] efficiency that we have in terms of cost collection and so on came in last year. And there, there was a significant drop outside of the number of days outstanding was confirmed and therefore the cash collection became far better. The second one is that we also have to take into account the extent of dividends that we have paid in July this year which included a special dividend. So, there was a cash outflow. The third one is that there has been a growth and this growth has demanded higher working capital. So, these are the three factors which have really

[worked it] and therefore when you do an analysis over a longer period and so on you'd find that there is a fair amount of efficiency in it. There is nothing to worry about here. KULDEEP KAUL: Okay. Thanks very much for that. And the third is this quarter again we had this write back of bad debt provisions. So, is that going to remain like that for a few quarters or is it going to revert back? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: We can do that in which case it will lessen our efforts. See bad-debt provisions go through a process of what we -- are very detailed by our process in terms of the write-backs and so on and obviously this is not something that you either plan for or you can hope to have it happen quarter after quarter. It's just that it turns out this way. KULDEEP KAUL: Okay. Thanks very much, guys. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Kaul. (Operator Instructions). Our next question is from the line of Edward Caso of Wells Fargo. Please go ahead. EDWARD CASO, ANALYST, WELLS FARGO: Good evening. Thanks for taking my question. I was interested in the trend in the tax rate, 18% or so now. I've seen others, it seems like the new level is settling out in the mid 20s, is that a level or is there something special in your tax structure that will keep you below that level? S. MAHALINGAM: You know in India we have had two provisions. One was in terms of what is called the 10A, which is the software technology tax, scheme and the other one is the SEZ which is 10AA. And 10A of course there is a 10-year rule and after that some of the units get out. So, in our case we have consciously planned from 2006 onwards that many of our extended businesses get into the SEZ facilities and so on. Probably they have done a very good way of planning that as compared to many of the competitors and so on. But, you will also notice that we have moved

from 15% to 18% and that has really happened because of units moving out of [STP] and so on. And as far as next year is concerned we have said that we'll be somewhere around 23% in terms of tax rate. EDWARD CASO: Thank you. My other question is on your view on local hiring, non-Indian hiring, in the US and in Europe, in particular, and what the implications are? How you're doing on that front, what impact it might have to your cost structure and so forth. Thank you. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Certainly we are continuing to recruit in the US and UK. And we will lag a little bit that. But I think the way our business model is structured we should be able to take care of that. Because when we [recruit] people from India, there is a significant cost. And by hiring people locally, I don't think that we -- it's going to pose a significant problem. EDWARD CASO: Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Caso. Our next question is from the line of Anurag Purohit of Alchemy. Please go ahead. ANURAG PUROHIT, ANALYST, ALCHEMY: Good evening to the management and thanks for taking my question. I have a question regarding the lateral recruitment which has been very strong since last three quarters in particular, much above the historical range. Is this a strategy in terms of expanding the employees in terms of span of control? Or is it something which is becoming normal in terms of incoming incremental projects that are coming into system? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes. We think that overall we would like to have from the planning point of view, more trainees and that is all we have planned now. But as the demand picked up and as there is growth, and we have to fulfill those requirements, as a result we have to go for laterals as well, because the entire project can't be stocked with trainees. So that is the reason why, given the growth to meet that demand, we had to hire laterals. And it's been about 54/46 ratio in favor

of laterals at this point in time and going forward that will come down and it will be more in favor of trainees rather than lateral. Maybe around 52/48 kind of ratio. ANURAG PUROHIT: Sure. Potentially that would mean that a pipeline will be very strong here in terms of share relation, if I'm not wrong. So are you seeing any kind of supply side issues when you're going to campus? Because as industry whole fresher recruitment is expected to be strong in the pipeline. If you could highlight what kind of pressure, salaries that you can offer for in pipeline and what kind of overall number you are looking for? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Okay. As far as potential salaries are concerned, it remains the same as what we have been doing so far. There is no change in the campuses. And as far as campus plan is concerned, we are planning to give around 37,000 offers. And out of that, 23,692 are already given out, from 171 institutes. And we still have a few institutes to go and we'll complete our campus by February, mid-February to end of February. ANURAG PUROHIT: And finally, one last bookkeeping question. In balance sheet this quarter, other non-current assets has witnessed a significant jump. Any particular reason for that? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No. No, there are no specific issues around non-current assets and so on. ANURAG PUROHIT: Okay. Thanks, and all the best. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Purohit. Our next question is from the line of Nitin Padmanabhan of Indiabulls Securities. Please go ahead. NITIN PADMANABHAN, ANALYST, INDIABULLS SECURITIES: Hi, thanks for taking my question. I think you had highlighted I think a quarter back or a couple of quarters back saying that by fourth quarter of 2012 we should have 10% incremental revenue from cloud-based offerings. Where are we on that today? And how do we see it going forward? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, we are on track for that. NITIN PADMANABHAN: Any sense you could give us in terms of what percent of the incremental it is today? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Why don't we wait

for next four quarters. NITIN PADMANABHAN: Sure. And anything with regard to pricing per se. I just want to understand, with Asset Leverage Solutions basically adding $17m odd incrementally this quarter, wouldn't that have had some sort of positive impact from a pricing perspective? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes and no. Yes and no. I think that if you are at 118 basis points, I don't think there is a pricing increase that has come -- you mean in a (inaudible) manner? NITIN PADMANABHAN: Also, would it be fair to assume that most of these are from scale-ups of non-top-10 clients, where a lot of scale-up has happened and were basically at higher rates? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: So I would say that it is a combination of three things. Contract renewals with the existing clients. There are a few. And some contracts which we closed in earlier quarters that are going still. And the third one is some solutions like the product business. NITIN PADMANABHAN: Right. And just one final question. What would be the yield on cash that we're getting right now? And is there scope for improvement going forward, considering that overall rates are going up? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: On the annualized basis it is 6.5%. And whether it will go up, yes, basically it's a question of putting it into a lot of these higher -yielding ones. After taking into account complete security, we don't invest in equity and all that kind of thing. So given that with those kind of interest patterns that we see in India, there could be a slight movement upwards. NITIN PADMANABHAN: Sure, perfect, thank you. And all the best. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Padmanabhan. Before we take the next question we'd like to request participants to please use handsets while asking a question. Our next question is from the line of Anthony Miller of TechMarketView. Please go ahead. ANTHONY MILLER, ANALYST, TECHMARKETVIEW: Yes, earlier in the call you alluded to the fact that growth in India and Latin America was really quite weak. In fact, when I looked at the numbers it looked like Latin

America in particular had quite a big hit, something like 15% down sequentially in dollar terms. And I'd just like to understand why that was. Was that one particular marketplace in Latin America, or even one particular client? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No, actually, as I said, we have seen declines in yields both in India and Latin America, because in these markets we have a higher proportion of discretionary projects and those projects came to an end. And we have not been able to get the follow-on projects, especially the quarter being the December quarter. So there was not enough discretionary project-based revenues. And that is a phenomenon both in India and in Latin America. And India also we had an exceptionally good quarter last quarter. There we grew almost 20% q-on-q. So given that, I think we have taken a hit in revenues in terms of growth rate. But it will start growing from here next quarter. ANTHONY MILLER: Okay, thank you. And my second question, just a data one. Can you give us the constant currency growth rates, please, for the total business and then for US and for Europe? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: For US and Europe it is 5.4%. Just wait a minute. Yes, we give 6.4% in US and Europe. And the constant currency overall growth rate is close to 6%. ANTHONY MILLER: Okay, thanks very much. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Miller. Our next question is from the line of Rahul Jain of Dolat Capital. Please go ahead. RAHUL JAIN, ANALYST, DOLAT CAPITAL: Yes, congrats. My question is pertaining to the telecom vertical where it is a lagger compared to the other verticals. What are sense in terms of the spending in the segment in the various geographies? How is spend happening on the upgradation, or setting up of new infrastructure? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: The particular reason for this quarter is because we had some discretionary projects in India that came to an end that were telecom vertical. That has resulted in the overall vertical showing a de-growth. On a sequential basis the international business (technical difficulty) has grown.

But having said that, we see opportunities in the telco sector, both in terms of upgrades and also in terms of people adopting to technologies like 3G. But albeit the growth in the telco will be lower than the other sectors. That's based on (technical difficulty). RAHUL JAIN: Okay. And just one more thing. As we've seen good growth in the solution or the banking product segment, what is the -- what is your perspective on the segment considering even peers are doing well in the space? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: As I said earlier on the call, we see good opportunities in the BFSI sector at this point. RAHUL JAIN: Okay, that's it from myself, thanks a lot. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Jain. Our next question is from the line of Subhashini Gurumurthy of Ambit Capital. Please go ahead. SUBHASHINI GURUMURTHY, ANALYST, AMBIT CAPITAL: Hi, thanks for taking my question. My question was pertaining to utilization levels. It's great that we've been able to maintain utilization despite the strong growth. But are we comfortable with the current rates? Or do we think they've peaked out at these levels? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Utilization level, excluding trainees, we have been able to retain at 83.8%. And whether -- can we do better than that? The possibility is always there. But at the same time, as we talked about at the last quarter, we are trying to improve our hiring so that we can create sufficient bandwidth and capacity. SUBHASHINI GURUMURTHY: So in that case, should we see utilization structurally at higher levels, in the sense of above 80% for the full year, for FY '12? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: FY '12 we still have to do our planning. But overall, yes, we would like to maintain our utilization levels at high level. SUBHASHINI GURUMURTHY: Sure. And just a question pertaining to the other income and it's actually doubled, the ForEx gains actually doubled over last quarter. Any reason behind that? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No, they actually this quarter, we have had a gain of about 52 crores as against loss last quarter. And therefore the swing has

been quite substantial, about 106 crores or so. SUBHASHINI GURUMURTHY: Yes, I'm talking about the dividend income excluding the ForEx. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: You're talking about the dividend income? Yes, you see, the problem that happens is that July/September quarter is that we have a lot of dividend outflows and so on. And therefore the amount of money that is kept in liquid funds in order to plan for that becomes a little bit higher therefore the yield even a little lower. Now this quarter we have had much greater -- much better (inaudible). SUBHASHINI GURUMURTHY: Sure, sure. And just one last question. What are the trends we are seeing on the vendor consolidation front? Are we still seeing incremental benefits from vendor consolidation? Or are you seeing this trend tapering off? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Well, we continue to see opportunities due to vendor consolidation. SUBHASHINI GURUMURTHY: Sure. Thanks a lot, that's all from my side. OPERATOR: Thank you, Miss Gurumurthy. Our next question is from the line of [Dipen Mehta] of SBI Cap Securities. Please go ahead. DIPEN MEHTA, ANALYST, SBI CAP SECURITIES: I guess in relation about our assurance service offering from last couple of quarters it is showing good growth. So what we are offering in that specific segment, service line? And secondly, I wonder about [that reason], what kind of trend we're seeing in the last two quarters, specifically? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: On assurance, this is an area where we see significant opportunity to continue to grow. The reason is that we are offering in the assurance space is very verticalized. So we are focused on developing assurance offerings for each industry and that is getting a lot of traction from the customers. And we are also using technologies like cloud and assurance. And in terms of attrition, I think Vandrevala will answer. PHIROZ VANDREVALA: In attrition, LPM-basis we are at 14.4% slightly over what we were in last quarter. But if I look at it from an absolute attrition point of view the total number of people that we lost this quarter, that is down by 13% as

compared to last quarter. So we have lost 13% less in the quarter to quarter. DIPEN MEHTA: Okay. So can you give us quarterly annualized numbers for current quarter? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Which one? DIPEN MEHTA: Quarterly and annualized -- N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I think I've given you all the numbers of gross addition, net additions, so from there you will be able to compute it. DIPEN MEHTA: Thank you. OPERATOR: Mr. Mehta, do you have any further questions? DIPEN MEHTA: No, thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you. Our next question is from the line of Madhu Babu of Systematix Shares & Stocks. Please go ahead. MADHU BABU, ANALYST, SYSTEMATIX SHARES & STOCKS: Yes, sir. So which are the services lines which will grow higher in the company average over the next two years? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: If I want to guide guidance (inaudible) I can't really find out. But as I said, services like infrastructure, assurance, even BPO, all these things -- ForEx, are all doing well. So I can't give you more specific. MADHU BABU: Sir, considering that infrastructure management, one of the competitors I've seen a bit of lower margin because of ramping up that service line. Do we take employees on our payroll? How are the margins in IMS deals? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: See, our business model is asset-light. Our business model is also more infrastructure management. So given that we don't take costs on our books, or assets on our books, in the way we deliver. So we are, as we scale in any contract, we will be able to deliver the right [models] and infrastructure, that sort of thing. PHIROZ VANDREVALA: And most of the people are employees, and we do use business associates just like any other business, when it's needed, when there are employees. MADHU BABU: Okay. And last question, sorry, APac has been seeing strong growth. Which are the geographies which are seeing stronger traction in APac region? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Australia is showing a lot of traction. And we hope to --- we are working on other focused markets like Japan, China and Singapore. Also those

are the key markets we look at in Asia. MADHU BABU: Okay, so thanks. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Babu. Our next question is from the line of Sandip Agarwal of Antique Stockbroking. Please go ahead. SANDIP AGARWAL, ANALYST, ANTIQUE STOCKBROKING: Yes, congratulations on a very good set of numbers. Just wanted to have the a little more detail on the products that is BaNCS. I just wanted to have more clarity on that, how is it doing, what is the kind of contribution it yielded in this quarter? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: The BaNCS business is doing very well. As I said, BaNCS is an integrated suite of offerings which has broad portfolio, apart from core Banking, payment, securities, custody, brokerage, market infrastructure, deposit, resettlement, (technical difficulty) and insurance offerings. And we are seeing a significant traction for all those offerings. And it's continuing to do better this quarter. It has contributed 4% of the company revenues compared to previous quarter where it contributed 3.4%. SANDIP AGARWAL: Okay. And one more question on the utilization levels. Just wanted to know will you be -- do you feel that this kind of utilization levels can be maintained going forward? And we will be able to bring down attrition levels for that? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: So if you are able to get the demand, yes, definitely. SANDIP AGARWAL: And [some] on attrition? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Yes, attrition is something that we are constantly working on. It's a top priority for the company. And continuing to focus people retention is a major initiative. Every manager is actively working on it. And we can -- it's still our intent it is very difficult to say. But we hope that that attrition levels will continue to decline. SANDIP AGARWAL: Okay. Thanks a lot. Thanks. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Agarwal. Our next question is from the line of Amar Mourya of Indianivesh Securities. Please go ahead. AMAR MOURYA, ANALYST, INDIANIVESH SECURITIES: Hi. Hello, sir. Congratulations for a good set of numbers. My question is relating to geography. I'd like to know, India and APac has seen a decline in this

particular quarter, any specific reason for this? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: APac has not shown a decline; only India has seen decline and Latin America has declined, as I said earlier in the call. It's primarily due to mix of discretionary projects and [needed] projects. These two markets have higher portion of discretionary compared to other markets. And the December quarter is always a difficult quarter for getting discretionary income. So these are reasons. AMAR MOURYA: Okay. And secondly, sir, I just wanted to know, like you had just mentioned, probably I missed out what is the gross additions for this quarter was? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: 20,209. AMAR MOURYA: Sorry, sorry? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: 20,209. AMAR MOURYA: 20,209. Okay, thank you, sir. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: (multiple speakers) OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Mourya. Our next question is from the line of Mitali Ghosh of Merrill Lynch. Please go ahead. MITALI GHOSH, ANALYST, BOFA MERRILL LYNCH: Good day, good evening, congrats on a good set of numbers. I dropped out somewhere in the middle of the call, so I apologize and just wanted to ask. I just wanted to understand what the large deal pipeline is looking like, and how many large deals were closed this quarter. And also the budgeting trend that you are seeing. So should we be expecting a stronger March quarter? Or directionally, how should we expect the March quarter to be? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thank you, Mitali. I think the large-deal pipeline looks good. We closed about nine deals this quarter across markets and verticals. And the pipeline is quite healthy. And from a spend point of view, what we are hearing from customers is that it will be -- at the current levels with an up-tick with some customers. So there'll be increasing spending with some customers, and then it looks good. MITALI GHOSH: Thanks. And just in terms of these nine deals that you closed. Any trend in terms of market verticals, or service line? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: No, it's very broad-based and across markets and industries, Mitali. MITALI GHOSH: Okay. So secondly just on the telecoms space, you mentioned you've seen

a growth sequentially in the international telecom area. Any sense of what kind of quantum you're talking about? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: It is mostly -- obviously it is lower single digit, Mitali, in international market. And the growth in the telecoms sector is continuing to be below the company average. And probably that trend will continue into the coming couple of quarters at least. MITALI GHOSH: Right. And just two quick questions. One is in terms of the pricing, what is the trend like in terms of offshore pricing and onsite pricing? I know you've given the combined 1.8, but any color in terms of offshore/onsite? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: We don't normally break the between onshore and offshore. And of overall, what I said is that the pricing has remained stable with an upward bias because the backlog being what it is, it's very difficult to put a number for pricing increase. But we have done well in seeing 118 basis points improvement in pricing. And that's all I'd like to say. MITALI GHOSH: Sure. So just lastly, just very quickly now, what is the hedging provision like now? PHIROZ VANDREVALA: We have not taken much of hedges as of December 31, subsequently as far as the coming quarter is concerned, we have done close to about $225m but very little. MITALI GHOSH: So this is with respect to revenue in March quarter? PHIROZ VANDREVALA: This is with respect to revenue of March quarter, that's right. MITALI GHOSH: Thank you. OPERATOR: Thank you, Miss Ghosh. Our next question is from the line of Pratik Gandhi of IDBI Capital. Please go ahead. PRATIK GANDHI, ANALYST, IDBI CAPITAL: Hi, thank you for taking my question. Margins, I wanted to understand, I think on SG&A we have seen a couple of line items like rent, travel and also the recruitment and training costs going up significantly in this quarter, by close to around 50% on rent side and 25%and 17% on the other two items So is it a specific phenomena? Or probably it will be normalized during next quarter? S. MAHALINGAM: You see the

thing is, that as far as rent is concerned, we have clarified last quarter that we had a dip last quarter because of certain arrangements that we had made that were supposed to be one-off. And therefore we are back to the same kind of [projections] we used to have before that. As far as travel is concerned, it has certainly gone up as I made in my initial statement as well. We tried to ensure that our cost-cutting does not imply killing any of the growth parameters and so on. But so we have continued to focus attention on that. So some of these costs that you are talking about are really those which are either normalizing because we had some benefit last quarter or just we continued to incur cost in that area rather than apply any of the cost reduction measures. PRATIK GANDHI: Right, Mahal, but I was talking about the SG&A rather the cost of revenue, where I think the rent cost has gone up by close to [56%]. S. MAHALINGAM: Even there, SG&A also we had certain benefits last quarter through restructuring. That's not restricted only to cost of revenue. PRATIK GANDHI: Okay. And secondly, any indication on the tax rate for the FY'13? S. MAHALINGAM: The FY'13, we said that we expected to be closer to 25%, which will be in line with the direct tax cost. PRATIK GANDHI: Okay. Thank you so much. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Gandhi. Our next question is from the line of Hardik Shah of KR Choksey. Please go ahead. HARDIK SHAH, ANALYST, KR CHOKSEY: Gentlemen, congratulations on a good set of numbers. Secondly, tell me how many percentage of our clients have closed their budget for calendar '11? N. CHANDRASEKARAN: I can't give you a percentage, but majority of them have closed, and I think the budgeting cycle of our clients are back to normal. So the majority of them are closed, but I'm not going to put a percentage. S. MAHALINGAM: And sir, do we expect any delay in their spending cycle? Because normally, clients start spending

in first quarter of calendar. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Generally, the discretionary projects spending will begin to happen towards the end of the quarter, first quarter. Even though budget is finalized, by the time it is allocated to people and the current (inaudible) takes a couple of months. So the discretionary spend for the next -- it will actually happen towards the end of the quarter, end of March. HARDIK SHAH: Just a final question, sir, in case of clients who have closed closed their budget, are you seeing any increase in discretionary spend or are you expecting the flat spending in discretionary areas? S. MAHALINGAM: I think the discretionary spend will be better in 2011 of compared to 2010. HARDIK SHAH: Okay, sir, thank you, thanks very much. OPERATOR: Thank you, Mr. Shah. Ladies and gentlemen, due to time constraints, that was the last question. I now hand the conference over to the management to add closing comments. N. CHANDRASEKARAN: Thank you, everyone, for joining the call. Overall, I would say that we have had a [turnover] good quarter. And the -- all operating parameters are looking good. And from the deal closures and the deal pipeline, the outlook looks healthy. And with that, I wish you all a very, very happy New Year. And see you next time. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, members of the management team and Mr. Shirali. Ladies and gentlemen, with that we conclude this conference call. Thank you for joining us, and you may now disconnect your line. Thank you. Q4 2017 Integra LifeSciences Holdings Corp Earnings Call - Final https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/q4-2017-integra-lifesciences-holdings-corp/docview/2013168532/se-2?accountid=27562 Abstract: None available. Full text: Presentation OPERATOR: Good day, and welcome to the Integra LifeSciences Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2017 Financial Results Conference Call. As a reminder, today's call is being recorded. At this time, I'd like to turn the conference over to Mike Beaulieu, Director of Investor Relations. Please go ahead, sir. MICHAEL BEAULIEU, IR, INTEGRA LIFESCIENCES HOLDINGS CORPORATION: Thank you, Audra. Good morning, and thank you for joining the Integra LifeSciences Fourth Quarter 2017 Earnings Conference Call. Joining me on the call are Peter Arduini, our President and Chief Executive Officer; and Glenn Coleman, our Chief Financial Officer and Corporate Vice President of International. And so think of that about half of it's in sitting in training and learning the new products and half of it is working with your new counterpart to do hand-offs of accounts. And so just that 2-week component alone has probably the largest effect on the pull-down on growth rate in the first quarter. ROBERT JUSTIN MARCUS: Great. And just as a follow-up, you have a key competitor who's, at least on the Wall Street side, going through some tough times with some investigations going on there. This is also a time when you're out hiring sales reps. So have you been able -- have you seen any increased ability to get the sales reps from your competitors here? And is there any chance for you to take advantage of their potential weakness on the market? PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes, I would just say like with all scenarios, there's ebbs and flows on competitors being in tough situation, which always plays out there. So we don't typically comment on it. I would just say when it comes down to opportunities, obviously, when we're in a growing state, particularly, in our product portfolios, we're going to be there for customers if they need the kind of support in the products that we can offer, so we'll be there. And I would say, as it comes to hiring, we're very much focused on our culture at Integra and finding the right individuals that fit the culture. If we find the right folks, we bring them in, but we don't bring in folks that may not fit our overall culture and that's a really important part of our hiring process as well. Thanks for your questions. OPERATOR: We'll go next to Craig Bijou at Cantor Fitzgerald. CRAIG WILLIAM BIJOU, RESEARCH ANALYST, CANTOR FITZGERALD & CO., RESEARCH DIVISION:

I want to start with the extremities number. And I think if I heard you correctly, Glenn, it was up 8% in Q4, and while lower extremities was down 5%. So that would suggest, just given the relative size, that ankle and shoulder were both very strong. So I wanted to see if you could provide maybe a little color there on what's driving that strength. I know you guys have been doing well for the past few quarters, but any additional color. GLENN G. COLEMAN: Yes. So your numbers are accurate. We grew 8% overall in extremities. If you look at just ankle and shoulder together, they grew about 25% with the faster growth coming from ankle. And so we're seeing really strong momentum with the Cadence launch, both in the U.S. and outside the U.S. So we're seeing really strong growth even in Europe with the Cadence launch and it's been very successful and that's driving a lot of the growth. And shoulder continues to put up double-digit quarters for us and part of that is continuing to add more distribution and distributors and part of it is we've got a really strong portfolio and some of the product launches we announced about 12 months ago are making a bit of difference there as well. Keep in mind, these are still relatively small parts of our business, but very fast-growing. And we're very focused on increasing the investment there. I will also say we've added, as we talked about in the past, a number of specialists like in the ankle space. I think that's making a big difference in terms of our growth as well. So we're quite excited now to have a dedicated channel that's just going to be focused on extremities metal, and we think that the growth can continue going forward, given the focus channel. PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes, I would just add the fact that over the last 18, 24 months, we have clearly made additional investments in the area. I think our R&D team, we really have the right group in place that

we've kind of struggled with in the past. We've got a very good clinical team. And I think you've heard us mention about our Austin consolidation where we really kind of huddled everybody, really coming to the realization that extremities weren't as different and you need to treat it that way. And so everything from our really world-class lab, Cadaver Lab, that we have and the locations to how we're creating this focused channel. I think we're well positioned here to kind of move the needle on these businesses. And as Glenn said, I think both shoulder and ankle really represent for us the biggest growth opportunities not only because they're the larger dollar amounts but we do think both those platforms were -- clearly have some of the best products out in the marketplace. CRAIG WILLIAM BIJOU: That's helpful. And then I'll stick with extremities and just a couple of follow-ups. One, on the total ankle DRG change, I know October 1 was the first day of the new DRG. So I wanted to see if you guys noticed any increase in volume due to that. And then just on -- I don't think you provided a specific 2018 how we should think about extremities growth when you listed -- when you went through the product segments, so just wondered if you had any color there on 2018 growth. PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes. Maybe I'll hit the DRG and then, Glenn, maybe you can hit the growth piece. So on the DRG, I would say, in the fourth quarter, we didn't necessarily see pure incremental, but I think what we have seen a lot is kind of evidenced by our training classes. Folks that were not -- were doing more fixation and not mobile-bearing work, arthroplasty, there's definitely an increase. The numbers there will make sense for most, if not all, institutions. So yes, we're clearly seeing more interest, more people wanting to come to our training, and hence, the focus and the alignment around ankle right now probably happens at a very good time for us. Glenn, do

you want to comment on the growth? GLENN G. COLEMAN: Yes. I'm not sure I want to give specific growth rates for all of our franchises for guidance. But we did indicate that OTT, in total, would be 8% to 10% organic growth. I would just say, relative to where we landed here in Q4, I would expect the growth for extremities to continue at least the pace where we exited Q4. Keep in mind, Q1 will be a bit of the down quarter, but then once we get past Q1, starting to see a pickup in the growth rates, probably somewhat higher than what we just had here in Q4. But that would exclude any of the channel changes, disruption and territory changes in Q1. But overall, I just think of it as an area of growth that should continue both in the back half of '18 as well as 2019 and beyond. OPERATOR: And we'll go next to Raj Denhoy at Jefferies. RAJBIR SINGH DENHOY, MD, EQUITY RESEARCH & SENIOR EQUITY RESEARCH ANALYST, JEFFERIES LLC, RESEARCH DIVISION: I wonder if I could maybe start with just the -- kind of the overall guidance for 2018. You -- to your point, you finished 2017 here on a pretty high note. You seem to be pretty pleased with how Codman's tracking. But you look at the specialties, the Codman Specialty Surgical guidance for 2018, it's 2%. I'm curious why so low in a sense. And I know there's going to be a little bit of disruption as you integrate Codman, but it sounds like things are tracking well there. Should we view that 2% as, perhaps, a conservative view on 2018 for that business? PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes, I'd say, Raj, I mean, the first part is, until we get through the integration, you don't know how good it is, right? I mean so yes, it's good at this point. Could we have some issues? There's the potential to have some issues and some of that's rolling off the TSAs, where J&J's supporting all of our order to cash and

order to transactions and such. And so we think we've got it nailed, but like in most things, we want to see the results come through before we increase it. And so one could view that as conservative. I would just say it's prudent, based on just where we are. And getting through the first quarter into April, May, so that we have all the training behind us, we have a clean month or 2 with numbers, is really what we, ultimately, want to see. But again, I'm pleased where we are at this point, it's in line with our plans. But we have plenty of things that we still have to climb through here, relative to the integration. RAJBIR SINGH DENHOY: Okay, fair enough. And then on the orthopedic tissue side, the sheer number of salespeople that you talked about at Analyst Meeting, I think 190 salespeople you want to add this year. I think the question was asked, but where are you in adding 190 salespeople? And when you think about just that number, as you're exiting 2018, with that much of an increase in the sales force, one would respect, again, that the growth will be significantly faster than where you're entering the year. So I don't know if there's any comments you can really make around that? PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes, I would just position it this way. So how you do the number count, we look at it on an FTE on a full-time equivalent. So obviously, if we had somebody that we hired last year and now we have the beginning of the year, they've got a full year versus a 6-month window, so when you think of the count. But think of it being as a little bit over halfway against that number is how to probably think about it. And a majority of those folks are really going to be coming on board here at the end of Q1, into Q2. So as in the second half, we should have a good quarter, quarter and a half of the full team on

board. Codman is, obviously, a little bit differently. We're pretty much full up right now, which is great. So all of those numbers are in fundamentally full capacity. I mean, we always have a few opens, but around the world, we're pretty much in good shape there. RAJBIR SINGH DENHOY: And then just one housekeeping. The divested and discontinued revenue was only $2.4 million I think in the quarter. The guidance for next year is $25 million. We've been thinking it was going to be a little higher this quarter. Was there just a timing issue in terms of some of the divested businesses or how should we think about that? PETER J. ARDUINI: Well, I mean, keep in mind, for the '18 window, we divested the Camino line and some other products, 4 products overall that were associated with the closing of the deal. So Glenn, you may want to talk about the breakout of... GLENN G. COLEMAN: Yes, Raj, so the discontinued products you see in the fourth quarter and then our guidance for 2018 represents the sales we're recognizing to Natus as part of a transition manufacturing agreement. So we're not essentially counting that in our organic growth; we're removing that, since it's temporary, as we work through the Natus divestiture. So that's what those revenues represent. In 2017 -- and obviously, the big part of that is the divested Camino products. So that's the bulk of '17 and '18 is really the TMA revenues to Natus. OPERATOR: We'll take our next question from Jonathan Demchick at Morgan Stanley. JONATHAN LEE DEMCHICK, EQUITY ANALYST, MORGAN STANLEY, RESEARCH DIVISION: Just 2 quick ones for me. One on, I guess, the tax benefits and the other on dural repair. On tax, obviously, you got a big tailwind, I mean, you talked about really, it sounds like you're dropping the majority of it through. Just given a lot of the investments that you plan to be making and ramping up some of the sales force reps, there's a lot of areas that you can invest in in shoulder, ankle, wound,

et cetera. Why was it the right decision right now to be dropping through, basically the entirety of the benefit, rather than investing some of it in maybe a faster sales ramp, or sales force ramp at various points or in a variety of R&D initiatives? GLENN G. COLEMAN: Yes, Jon, it's a good question. When we look at the investments that we need to make in 2018, I would just highlight that back when we provided our initial guidance, we did indicate incremental investments of about $0.07 that were going into the OTT channel. So we had included that in our initial guidance and we're still on track to our spending plan. So while there's always opportunity to spend more money in R&D, clinical studies, continuing to hire even more reps, we felt like we had factored in enough of an expense increase to support our plans for 2018. Obviously, there were some things we wanted to do around our capital structure to lock in interest rates over the next 5 years, given where we thought rates were going to go and we took the opportunity to do some of that here in the fourth quarter. So obviously, that's going to result in kind of a onetime cost to execute the swaps, but the timing was quite good. I mean, we locked in in December, where the yield curves were still relatively low and they've obviously shot up since January and February. So we're quite happy that we did that. But overall, given where we were for our spending plan, we felt like it was appropriate to let about half of that tax benefit flow through to the EPS for the year and get us to the $2.35 number now for 2018. JONATHAN LEE DEMCHICK: Understood, very clear. And then just a quick question on dural repair. Obviously, over the last couple of quarters -- or this year, it's been a little more challenged, but certainly in the fourth quarter, it popped up a bit. It sounds like the expectations are relatively flat into 2018. I mean, I know

you gave a little bit of the rationale, but I was wondering if there was a little more color you could provide us. It just seems that there's momentum back in the business following the changes. To see why it would necessarily decelerate that much is just a little bit confusing to me. PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes, I would say, think about it this way, Jon. I think for the year, I mean, we're expecting a pickup in the mid- to low single-digits area. But I think in the first quarter, it's really tied to the disruption component. Dural repair and access is driven by having reps in the operating room with the neurosurgeon, that's the typically highest correlation to what -- how we see the growth moving. And that's going to be down some just based on my comments about out of the field. Now as you go into Q2, Q3 and Q4, the opportunity to grow counts, the opportunity to sell in a larger overall bundle and the amount of increased reps, that's how we're going to see the uptick. So I think you take Q1 out of the mix, expect in the range of 3% or so to probably be the kind of growth range in the outer quarters. OPERATOR: We'll go next to Matt Taylor at Barclays. MATTHEW CHARLES TAYLOR, DIRECTOR, BARCLAYS BANK PLC, RESEARCH DIVISION: So I wanted to follow up on the thread on organic growth here. I guess when you look at the segment organic growth for this year, the 2% versus the 8% to 10%, you gave slightly different ranges for those segments in the long-term outlook last year. And I guess I was wondering, especially on the 2% for Codman, is that lower this year because you're accounting for some potential disruption and your longer-term view has not changed from, I think, it was 3% to 5% before? Or is there something else that is different? PETER J. ARDUINI: No, Matt, that's exactly right. I think we believe that an underlying couple points growth business, we can double or triple that

over the long run with the investments in channel, R&D and all that. But starting the clock here the beginning of the year with changing fundamentally everyone's territories, taking them out of the field for a couple of weeks and then starting to come off of transition services agreements in the second quarter, there's going to be disruption. So that's been factored in and the result of it is more of a muted organic growth rate, particularly, within the first half. That being said, if we can navigate these waters and execute them with better-than-planned lower disruption, there's clearly upside potential as we get into the second half. MATTHEW CHARLES TAYLOR: Okay. All right. That's helpful. And maybe just because you are talking about the changes in the channel in both segments, could you kind of compare and contrast for us what's going on functionally differently in each one? Or is the process relatively similar, even though, obviously, one of them is bringing in more inorganically? PETER J. ARDUINI: Well, I mean, the channel splits are actually -- I would say, the process and the thinking and the analytics using the right kind of data and geo-mapping and all those kind of things were very similar to try to figure out how to minimize disruption. But the output was quite different. I mean, in many cases, with a Codman and a Integra integration, it was about we have more people than we need, who has a seat and who doesn't and where are they. And so we went through that in Q4. And internationally, fundamentally, it was all key performers have a seat. So making sure that we map territories the right way and train folks. And so as I previously said, we're fundamentally through most of the training and now it's about executing in a new territory. The OTT is a little bit different from the standpoint of actually taking a legacy Integra rep that just had a bag that got too large and too diverse, and then figuring out who were the best at selling tissue, who were

the best at mental, and dividing it, and knowing that as you divide that, you're going to have some deficits, meaning open territories. So we have a little bit more of a crossover coverage for a longer period of time as we get those territories backfilled. But we're on track to that. And again, part of that is making sure that you're open, transparent and compensate your sales reps appropriately for the work they're doing. And so it has a little bit more of a different approach to it on OTT than CSS, but both of them are out of the blocks well and I think on track to what we want to achieve. GLENN G. COLEMAN: And, Matt, maybe to put a finer point to some of Pete's comments, to put it into context. We've probably had our reps out of the field 12 to 13 days this year versus historically 3 to 4 days, just to put that into context around a lot of comments that Pete's making. So that's really, when we talk about the disruption factor and the new territories and so forth, what we're dealing with in the first quarter. OPERATOR: We'll move to our next question from Steven Lichtman at Oppenheimer & Company. STEVEN M. LICHTMAN, MD AND SENIOR ANALYST, OPPENHEIMER & CO. INC., RESEARCH DIVISION: Codman, obviously, accelerated your international footprint plan by years. Can you talk about how you're seeing cross-selling opportunities outside of the U.S., how they'll build here throughout '18 and into 2019? PETER J. ARDUINI: Steve, I'll let our Head of International comment on that. GLENN G. COLEMAN: Yes, so Steve, it's still early days, but we've already identified several opportunities and closed several opportunities, cross-selling, specifically, in Europe between the 2 teams. I think that's really where we see a lot of value of this deal is the cross-selling opportunities. So some successes, so far early days in Europe. I'm expecting more around the globe, as we move into 2018. But clearly, we're quite excited to now have the broadest portfolio in neurosurgery being sold through the largest

channel. And I'll continue to expect more opportunities closing here as we get into 2018. But it's still very early days. And I can tell you in the first quarter alone, so far, we've closed a couple of nice opportunities between the 2 teams. Yes, and then look forward, I would just continue to emphasize, the big opportunities outside the U.S. for our teams is really in Asia Pacific. As we've talked about in the past, our business in Asia doubled overnight with the Codman acquisition. And Japan, really exciting opportunities, we've now got a very significant sales force there. We've got several new products for launching in the back half of '18, including our flagship product DuraGen. CUSA Clarity we'll launch towards the tail end of 2018. And China, our team doubled in China and we have put up very significant growth rates in China as well. And so for me, as I look towards the back half of '18 and go into '19, to your point, the cross-selling opportunities should continue to accelerate. PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes. And, Steve, I think one of the things we've been acutely focused on, the old Drucker comment that culture eats strategy for breakfast, the culture of the 2 companies is very good, and that's a big deal for us. As we're expanding around the world to have J&J-trained individuals in all these different countries, if we would have done it ourselves with different small acquisitions, that homogeneity, so to speak, of kind of beliefs and ideas is a big deal and I think both of our teams, in most recent training I kind of gauge it by as I'm walking around, can I identify which side of the house were the reps from and by the end of the training, it's extremely difficult to see were you a legacy Integra or Codman individual, which gets to the point that the teams are blending very well. And I use that as a bigger barometer more than anything, just because the cultural component and do people feel comfortable, do they feel like they're

in place they want to be, we feel very, very good about how the fit is with Codman. STEVEN M. LICHTMAN: Okay, great. And then just secondly, just wanted to get a sense of how far along you are in the CUSA upgrade cycle overall. Are we still in the middle innings here at this point? And is this still a potential tailwind for more than a 12-month period ahead as you look at the funnel? PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes, look, it's early days. It's early days in an installed base that has many years in front of us for conversion. Obviously, what could change the trajectory of that is what competitors may come into the market and do they bring technologies that rival what we have with CUSA Clarity. We think there's really nothing in the marketplace today that rivals it. So we think there's clearly a replacement and a competitive socket-win opportunity for the next few -- 2 to 3 years, for sure. OPERATOR: We'll go next to Jayson Bedford at Raymond James. JAYSON TYLER BEDFORD, SENIOR MEDICAL SUPPLIES AND DEVICES ANALYST, RAYMOND JAMES & ASSOCIATES, INC., RESEARCH DIVISION: So you mentioned several sizable orders in the fourth quarter on Specialty Surgical. Can we assume $2 million, $3 million? Is that in the ballpark? GLENN G. COLEMAN: Total of $2 million, total. JAYSON TYLER BEDFORD: $2 million. GLENN G. COLEMAN: Total, right. JAYSON TYLER BEDFORD: Okay. And then in terms of, and I apologize if this is in the deck here, but $5 million in other income in the fourth quarter, what was that due to, Glenn? GLENN G. COLEMAN: So keep in mind, the $5 million, we adjust out about $3 million of that in our adjusted EPS, which is the gain on the sale of Camino. The other $2 million is income associated with the cross-currency swaps, which I mentioned. We saw about a $2 million benefit in the fourth quarter and that's a benefit that will continue in 2018 and beyond. PETER J. ARDUINI: Of a couple million a quarter. GLENN G. COLEMAN: Couple million

dollars a quarter, yes. JAYSON TYLER BEDFORD: Okay. And so, as I look at your interest expense guide, the $75 million to $80 million, does that include the benefit of the swap? Is that a net number or gross? GLENN G. COLEMAN: No. JAYSON TYLER BEDFORD: Okay. So interest expense is $75 million to $80 million. Other income is at least $8 million based on this swap? GLENN G. COLEMAN: I would model $7 million for the full year 2018 in other income. Somewhere between $75 million to $80 million on the interest expense line. JAYSON TYLER BEDFORD: Okay. And that's helpful. And then the last one for me. Gross margin in the fourth quarter was a little softer than we expected. The '18 guide calls for 100 to 200 basis point improvement from that fourth quarter level. Can you just comment on kind of the softness in the fourth quarter and then kind of what improves in '18? GLENN G. COLEMAN: Yes, so keep in mind, there was a couple of factors that drove the fourth quarter lower and it was combination of Puerto Rico and the manufacturing costs of Puerto Rico, we had unabsorbed cost there. That will get better, obviously, as we ramp production and we're expecting to see a much better gross margin picture in Puerto Rico, really starting in the second quarter when there's favorable variances start rolling through the P&L. So Puerto Rico is a driver. Keep in mind, a lot of the overachievement in the fourth quarter was from lower gross margin products like private label, like the Derma Sciences products. And so the mix was unfavorable relative to what we've seen in the past. So we're counting on an improved mix here as we go into 2018 as well where we're expecting some of the growth, especially in the regenerative products. So I would just really contribute it to mix and Puerto Rico manufacturing variances improving in 2018. OPERATOR: And we'll go next to Matt O'Brien at Piper Jaffray. WILLIAM GEORGE INGLIS, RESEARCH ANALYST, PIPER JAFFRAY COMPANIES, RESEARCH DIVISION: This is Will

Inglis on for Matt. First off, a quick question on wound care. If you could provide any color on just kind of the adoption and utilization rates there, and then I guess more specifically with Omnigraft, has that been meeting expectations? Is it lighter than where it could be with more reimbursement? Any information there would be helpful. PETER J. ARDUINI: Yes. Well, I mean, wound care did quite well. We had a strong performance in the fourth quarter I think. Our strategy, particularly, in the second half of the year, was leveraging our amniotic platform, which had a very good performance as well as our PriMatrix product. I would say, we're still not happy with the performance that we achieved with Omnigraft. Now some of that we baked in strategically because of the characteristics of the product that it actually heals with less applications isn't necessarily compatible with CMS reimbursement to date. We have plans in place for the future that we think can change that. But clearly, that's not contributing to the level that we'd like to see, so that would be the soft spot. But when you look across the broader portfolio with our offloading product, the EZ cast, the MEDIHONEY products, PriMatrix, AMNIOEXCEL, all actually did quite well and are continuing to grow sequentially, at a level, I would say, that we're pretty happy with. WILLIAM GEORGE INGLIS: Great. And a quick follow-up. Just looking at your extremities sales force kind of restructuring, have you seen any disruptions in terms of customer relationships now that there's more kind of case coverage associates involved? And if not, are you helpful -- are you expecting that structure to be helpful going forward? PETER J. ARDUINI: Well, I mean, so we've got some case disruption, but it's been pretty minimal and we've been able to actually, at this point in time, deal with it, but that's some of our cautiousness relative to the growth rates in the fourth quarter, exactly that. And to your point, long term, we feel very confident that a focus channel that has just more

time to dedicate themself to a given clinical area, will return higher results, both in our wound reconstruction, our outpatient wound, our surgical products and extremities orthopedics, which in OTT now, all have focus channel areas. And so for sure, that is clearly an organic growth lever on the future for us. OPERATOR: And that does conclude the question-and-answer session. I'll turn the conference back over to management for any closing remarks. PETER J. ARDUINI: Just like to say thank you for joining the call and all the good questions, and I'll see many of you here, I think, at different road shows, events over the next couple of months. Thanks for joining the call. GLENN G. COLEMAN: Thank you. OPERATOR: And that does conclude today's conference. Again, thank you for your participation. Q1 2017 MiMedx Group Inc Earnings Call - Final https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/q1-2017-mimedx-group-inc-earnings-call-final/docview/1898074995/se-2?accountid=27562 Abstract: None available. Full text: Presentation OPERATOR: Good day, ladies and gentlemen, and welcome to the MiMedx Group Inc. Q1 2017 Earnings Conference Call. (Operator Instructions) As a reminder, this conference call is being recorded. I would now like to introduce your host for today's conference, Thornton Kuntz, SVP of Administration. Please go ahead. THORNTON A. KUNTZ, SVP OF ADMINISTRATION, MIMEDX GROUP, INC.: Thank you, operator, and good morning, everyone. This presentation contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of Section 27A of the Securities Act of 1933 and Section 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. These statements are based upon the current beliefs and expectations of our management and are subject to risks and uncertainties. Actual results may differ materially from those set forth in, contemplated by, or underlying the forward-looking statements based on factors described in this conference call and in our reports filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, including our Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2016, and our most recent 10-Q. We do not undertake to update or revise any forward-looking statements, except as maybe required by the company's disclosure obligations and filings it makes with the Securities and Exchange Commission under federal securities laws. With that, I will turn the call over to Pete Petit, MiMedx's Chairman and CEO. PARKER H. PETIT, CHAIRMAN AND CEO, MIMEDX GROUP, INC.: Good morning. Thank you for joining us for our first quarter of 2017 conference call.
