OPERATOR: Bill Herbert, Simmons & Co. BILL HERBERT, ANALYST, SIMMONS & CO.: Thanks, good morning. Paal and I guess Simon as well, in light of your commentary, Paal, with regard to a transition in pressure pumping pricing in North America likely at hand, how should we think about the 2012 capital spending budget at this juncture? Is it likely to be up year-over-year or what is the likely path on that front? PAAL KIBSGAARD: So if you look at our original plans for 2012, we were looking to add from the 2011 levels. We are maintaining our capacity to add both from a supplier and from a manufacturing standpoint. I am not talking about global CapEx. To what extend we will add I think is going to be a function of what comes out of the budgeting cycle of

our customers. But generally, we are maintaining our capacity to add CapEx going into 2012. BILL HERBERT: All right. So capital spending translation is likely higher year-over-year? PAAL KIBSGAARD: It generally will be higher. To what level higher, I think we will come back to you in a quarter's time. BILL HERBERT: Understood. Thank you. And then secondly, you guys I think have been sensibly balanced, if you will, with regard to the evolution of the development of the international unconventional resource. And yet you read through this release and you have more than a few wins here with regard to what is going on in Poland and Argentina and using HiWAY in North Africa, what have you. Do you have an update with regard to what your expected timeline is with regard to the blossoming of the unconventional play? And is it evolving more quickly than what you thought maybe a couple of quarters ago? PAAL KIBSGAARD: I think we have always been, I would say, positive on the outlook for unconventional resources internationally. If you look at some of the reports coming back, the estimated resource base for unconventional gas overseas is 6 times that of the US. So I think we have always been positive on that. I think the main thing we have been saying is firstly that we are going to have to go through a phase of exploration and appraisal to understand these resources internationally. And I think also the approach to how they are going to be developed are going to be different than what we do in North America and more in line with what Andrew just described, which is focused on a more scientific approach whether it is more upfront investment in the characterization. And also there is going to have to be focus on using less resources for the drilling and completions of these wells as well. So we are actively pursuing all these exploration and appraisal projects in all parts of the world whether this is Argentina, Colombia, Canada, Poland, India and China and so forth. So we are

I think very well-placed to play a strong role in the international shale market. I think the volume of activity is still going to take some time before it gets there, but I think there has been encouraging results from the exploration and appraisal projects. So I think the outlook is positive, but it is not going to be as significant as what we have seen in North America over the past four or five years in the coming years at least. BILL HERBERT: Okay, and if I could slip one in here with Simon. Simon, do you have handy what the sequential revenue increase was in US and Canada respectively? SIMON AYAT: Well, of course, I have them, but I mean you have seen the 15% overall NAM increase and we will leave it at that. I am not going to separate it between Canada and the US, but Canada had a significant increase obviously because of the spring break-up. BILL HERBERT: Okay, thank you. OPERATOR: Jim Crandell, Dahlman Rose. JIM CRANDELL, ANALYST, DAHLMAN ROSE: Good morning. Andrew and Paal, you spoke very positively about the outlook for deepwater. And one of your -- I believe one of your competitors said the most competitive pricing part of the market was in deepwater and on longer contracts. And you talked about some improvement. Are you actually seeing improvement in the deepwater area in terms of pricing or is that an area that has not really shown improvement in pricing and you are getting it somewhere else? PAAL KIBSGAARD: Well, if you look at the deepwater, if you look at the deepwater pricing, I would say it is still generally competitive, but I think what we see on the deepwater side is that there is a continued, I would say, value put on our technology and the integrity that we have over our operations. So in some cases, we are able to get a pricing premium for the performance that we are able to offer up on these very highly expensive rigs. There has also been an increase draw on the

high-end services that we offer up as part of the contracts on these rigs. So I think overall pricing on deepwater I am quite happy with. JIM CRANDELL: Okay and Paal, what -- different subject -- but what new or recent technology introduction with Schlumberger, let's say over the last year to 18 months, do you feel will have the biggest commercial impact on the Company in 2012? PAAL KIBSGAARD: Well, I don't know. If I single out one, then I am going to get a lot of my segments upset, so I'm going to have to be careful with that. But I would say that if I look at some of the key introductions that we have done over the past year or so, I would say, for wireline, I have a lot of hope and faith in the dielectric scanner. I think what this can offer up in terms of high-end petrophysical evaluation is going to be good. I referenced in my prepared remarks several D&M technologies -- PowerDrive Archer, MicroScope, SonicScope. These are all, I think, going to have -- they are going to create very good value for Drilling and Measurement. We have also introduced several new drill bit technologies, in particular the spare bit, which is focused in on unconventionals, which I think is going to be good. And obviously HiWAY, without saying, has a very significant impact today. And I think as we continue to widen the customer base for HiWAY, it is going to have a very significant impact on pressure pumping both in North America and overseas. And then one other key technology, which again Andrew referenced, is the UniQ land seismic. This is a single sensor land seismic system where we can basically house up to 100,000 channels and it's going to give us a lot better quality images for both structure and also for (inaudible) properties. And then the last part, which I also referenced in my prepared remarks, is our abilities now in terms of exploration software and petroleum system modeling. These are some of the key technologies that

we have introduced, which I have a lot of faith in going forward. JIM CRANDELL: Okay, and one quick one for Andrew. Andrew, after the elections in Mexico, would you expect drilling in Chicontepec to come back significantly and if so, what sort of form do you think the contracts could take over there? ANDREW GOULD: Look, I don't think that the elections have a huge impact on spending in Chicontepec. What will have an impact on spending in Chicontepec, and so far, we don't seem to have a clear case, is a method of improving the recovery from this reservoir, which, as you know, is extremely difficult. And while there are lots of different experiments going on, I don't think there is a conclusion on it yet. So I don't think the election will have any effect. JIM CRANDELL: Okay. So you really would not expect at this point Chicontepec to come back to any meaningful extent in 2012? ANDREW GOULD: Only if Pemex is satisfied that one of the technologies that they are applying to improve recovery is really working. JIM CRANDELL: Got it. Okay, thank you. OPERATOR: Angie Sedita, UBS ANGIE SEDITA, ANALYST, UBS: Great. Thanks, guys, good morning. Clearly, the international and deepwater recoveries is far and away the bigger story for Schlumberger. But on North America, you had touched on the uncertainty in pressure pumping pricing. Given the capacity that we are adding today and next year, do you think in a flat rig market in the US that we could reach a balanced or oversupply scenario in 2012? And then your thoughts on your North American margins in a flat rig market in 2012. PAAL KIBSGAARD: So in terms of trying to predict when we get into horsepower balance in terms of supply and demand, I am not going to do that. Obviously with a flatter rig count, it is likely to happen sooner, but I am not going to venture out and say exactly when that is going to happen. I think the impact on pricing will be a function of when it

happens, but it is also going to be a function of when we think it is going to happen. So I think you will have impact on pricing even before you actually get to saturation. If you look at our plans with that backdrop, we are, at this stage, continuing to add horsepower and we will continue to add horsepower until we see an actual change in the market. That being either a change to our customer plans or a change to the utilization of our assets. And we haven't really seen anything in terms of these changes yet, but we are continuing to add and the reason for this is that we are actually looking to gain pressure pumping marketshare and we are looking to gain share through our HiWAY technology and also by bundling our pressure pumping with the shale Reservoir Characterization workflow that we have highlighted. So we are continuing to add the function of when the supply demand is going to meet in horsepower. I am not going to make a prediction of when it is going to happen. It will eventually happen; I think that's what I would say. ANGIE SEDITA: Okay. And then a follow-up to that. So the horsepower that you're adding, are your plans only for the first six months of the year or is it so far for the full year and then how much of your frac fleet is under term today? PAAL KIBSGAARD: Well, we have a relative flexible way of handling how we add resources. So we have a very good relationship with our suppliers here, so we are looking out in various time horizons. So I am not going to say exactly how we are going to add quarter by quarter, but we have capacity to add throughout next year if we choose to. In terms of what power contracts we have on term, there is still a good mix between spot market and longer-term contracts. ANGIE SEDITA: Okay. And then touching back on the fourth quarter, you touched on revenues, but a little bit on margins.

Thoughts on North America with the seasonality going into Q4? Could you see some modest margin pressure just through the holidays? And then thoughts on your international margins for Q4. In Q3, you were at 18%. With the seasonal jump in Q4, could you move to the 20% range? PAAL KIBSGAARD: Well, I'm going to have to give you the same answer I gave to Ole. We have already ventured further out than what we typically do in indicating what is going to happen for the next quarter. So I would just reiterate that the EPS consensus estimate at this stage is probably on the optimistic side. ANGIE SEDITA: Okay. And then finally, in the international markets, is there any market that you are concerned may be slow or sluggish versus the areas that you have pointed out that should be quite strong in 2012? PAAL KIBSGAARD: No, I think in general not. We have indicated in the Q2 call that the growth in international markets is not going to be linear. So you will always potentially have an off quarter in some of these markets, but generally, I don't have any particular concerns in any part of the international market in terms of the growth potential. ANGIE SEDITA: The UK North Sea should be --? The outlook for the UK North Sea should be okay, flat, not down. ANDREW GOULD: Angie, it's Andrew. Can I remind you of our rules? It is one question and one follow-up only. And we do need to get a last question in. Thank you, Angie. OPERATOR: Geoff Kieburtz, Weeden & Co. GEOFF KIEBURTZ, ANALYST, WEEDEN & CO.: Thanks very much. Paal, just to clarify a comment I think you made during your prepared comments that pressure pumping pricing has flattened in the liquids market and declined in the gas regions. If that is right, then pressure pumping prices fell in the third quarter. Can you clarify what you said earlier? PAAL KIBSGAARD: I was saying that the pressure pumping pricing in the liquids-rich basins was flattening. GEOFF KIEBURTZ: Flattening. Okay. PAAL KIBSGAARD: Flattening

during the quarter, but we saw a modest drop in prices in the gas basin. GEOFF KIEBURTZ: So overall, pressure pumping prices still climbed in the third quarter? PAAL KIBSGAARD: They climbed slightly, yes. GEOFF KIEBURTZ: Okay, all right. And not to be too cute, Schlumberger has forecasted rolling over of pressure pumping margins and pricing several times in the past. And I understand your point that it will happen at some point, but are you seeing things, and perhaps it is just what you just said, the pricing that makes you more certain that it is going to happen and it is going to happen in a reasonably near future? PAAL KIBSGAARD: So again, I am not saying that it is going to happen in the near future. I am just saying that, during the third quarter, we saw flattening of the liquids-rich basin pricing, which we haven't seen in recent quarters. And I am also saying that there is a significant amount of horsepower coming into the market as we speak and also going into next year. So my concern on the North America outlook is what is going to happen to pressure pumping pricing. GEOFF KIEBURTZ: Right, okay. And on HiWAY, kind of a technical question I guess. My recollection was, at the analyst event this year, that, at that time, HiWAY wasn't delivering a reduction in water usage, but now clearly it is. Is HiWAY -- should we be thinking about HiWAY as still an evolving and developing technology? PAAL KIBSGAARD: I think absolutely. So whether you reduce only proppant or whether you reduce proppant, water and potentially horsepower is a function of how you design your jobs and it is also a function of what type of jobs, what conventional type of jobs you are replacing. So that is one thing. But I think to your second point, it is clearly an evolving technology. We are putting a lot of effort into the application range of HiWAY and we are also putting a lot of effort into how we design the jobs to make it

even more effectful in terms of how it can reduce resources and basically provide more production for less. So yes, it is clearly evolving and I am actually very optimistic with the traction we are now getting with the customer base and with any new technology and I would say also maybe in particular in North America land, it takes time to break through and penetrate the market with a new technology. And I think we are starting to get some good traction. GEOFF KIEBURTZ: And if I could squeeze a third one in here, please, as I am the last, I will just take the advantage. But on the international pricing, the text says early signs of pricing traction specifically for Wireline and D&M. This is it seems on the surface to be quite similar to the comments that have been made in prior quarters. Can you point to something specific that has changed or is it just that we continue to be in this state that it looks imminent? PAAL KIBSGAARD: I think what we said in earlier quarters is that we expected to see pricing traction towards the end of this year; that is what we said earlier. And what I am saying this quarter is that we are actually seeing some signs of it. There are really two signs I would highlight. First of all, higher demand and higher sales of our high-end technology, so there is a lot more salable technology from basic technology to high-end. And secondly, we are also testing pricing on specific contracts, typically smaller contracts, with reasonable success. GEOFF KIEBURTZ: Great. Thank you very much. MALCOLM THEOBALD: On behalf of the Schlumberger management team, I would like to thank you for participating in today's call. Keeley will now provide the closing comments. OPERATOR: Thank you. Q2 2004/2005 Satyam Computer Services Limited Earnings Conference Call - Final https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/q2-2004-2005-satyam-computer-services-limited/docview/467017675/se-2?accountid=27562 Abstract: [Ramalinga Raju]: Let me add to what [V. Full text: OPERATOR: Good morning, ladies and gentlemen. I am Pratibha, the moderator for this conference. Welcome to the Satyam conference call hosted by SSKI Securities. For the duration of the presentation, all participants' lines will be in the listen-only mode. After the presentation, the question-and-answer session will be conducted for participants connected CyberBazaar International bridge. After that there would be a Q&A session for participants connected to the SingTel bridge; and at the end, a Q&A session for participants connected to CyberBazaar India bridge. I would now like to turn over the call to Mr. Surendra Goyal of SSKI Securities. Thank you and over Mr. Goyal. SURENDRA GOYAL, ANALYST, OF SSKI SECURITIES: Yeah, thank you Pratibha. Good day everyone. This is Surendra Goyal from SSKI Securities. I welcome you all to the Satyam earnings call. Joining us on the call is the Satyam management from Santa Clara. We have with us Mr. Ramalinga Raju, Chairman; Mr. Ram Mynampati, who is the President, Commercial and Healthcare businesses; Mr. V. Srinivas, the CFO; and Dr. Prakash Challa, the CEO of Nipuna. I would now like to hand over the call to Mr. Srinivas, over to you Srinivas. V. SRINIVAS, CFO, SATYAM COMPUTER SERVICES LTD.: Thank you, Surendra. Good morning and good evening to you all and thank you for joining us to discuss the results of the quarter ended September 30, 2004. Before we start the discussion, I would like to draw your attention to the fact that during this call we may make certain forward-looking statements concerning our future growth prospects. Such statements involve a number of risks and uncertainties associated with our business. Please refer to our various periodic filings with SEC for a

description of such risks. The Company does not undertake to update any forward-looking statement that may be made from time to time by or on behalf of the Company. I'll now handle the session to Raju. RAMALINGA RAJU, CHAIRMAN, SATYAM COMPUTER SERVICES LTD.: Thank you, Srinivas. Good morning and thank you for joining us on this call. I'm pleased to report that the Company achieved a 9.93% sequential revenue growth in Q2 on the back of a continued business momentum with the existing customers. Revenues for Q2 at rupees 848.10 crores was better than the guidance of rupees 810 crores. The strong growth in revenue was accompanied by margin expansion on account of continued rationalization in selling and general administrative expenses. EPS at rupees 5.95 grew 8.58% sequentially, higher than the guidance of rupees 5.48. On account of positive business demand witnessed in Q2, we are revising our revenue guidance upwards for fiscal 2005 to rupees 3415-3428 crores implying an annual growth of 34.4-34.9%. EPS for fiscal 2005 is also revised upwards to rupees 22.61-22.76. The broad-based growth across verticals is a noteworthy feature of our performance in Q2. The threshold revenue limit per entry into top 10 has increased by 87% compared to the corresponding quarter last fiscal, indicative of our enhanced intimacy with existing customers. Our verticalized focus and business solution-centric approach has contributed to an impressive growth of 38%, traditionally strong vertical of the BSFI. Our Telecom vertical has also recorded a robust growth rate of 18%, thus sustaining double-digit growth recorded in the previous two quarters. We continue to experience accelerated growth in newer verticals; Healthcare, for instance, has registered a 25% sequential growth. When we have observed enhanced opportunities in enterprise solutions, infrastructure services are also showing a lot of promise. We believe that managed infrastructure services address an important element of IT services value chain and compliment our efforts to be a truly end-to-end solution provider. We are also pleased with our leadership position in integrated engineering solution practice with an associate strength in excess of 1,700. This breadth of service offerings, in our

opinion, would enable us to compete well with global systems integrator. We're aggressively pursuing our expansion strategy to explore new markets and to partner with our global customer base across different geographies. We are upbeat about the business potential in China and are expanding our presence there. We opened our largest global development center outside India, in Melbourne, Australia, which will serve as a major technological development and software support center for Asia-Pacific operations. In the next few weeks, our European operations are being strengthened with the addition of a development center in Hungary. It will help us address opportunities for near shore services, provide access to East European markets. We are excited about the prospects in Latin American market and plans are underway to anchor our presence in Sao Paulo to address these markets. To support this strategy, we are laying strong emphasis on building a robust Satyam brand and on developing seamless global delivery capabilities. We believe this will help us in bringing about higher levels of efficiencies for our customers and reinforce the mutually beneficial partnership that we have established. Nipuna, our BPO subsidiary recorded revenue of Rupees 11.05 crores in Q2. Nipuna has made significant investments in building world-class infrastructure facilities. The Company has 1,350 associates and is providing services to 16 customers. I am happy to state that the Board has considered an interim dividend of 100% for fiscal 2005. I now request Srinivas to comment on the financial highlights. V. SRINIVAS: Thank you, Raju. During Q2, our software revenues grew by 9.93% over the previous quarter. Entire growth was driven by volume increase with prices showing marginal improvement over last quarter. Offshore prices were at US $22.85 per hour and onsite rates were at US $55.59 per hour. EBITDA margins improved marginally to 26.03% in Q2 from 25.77% in the previous quarter, primarily driven by [technical difficulty]. Looking at the market dynamics and improving demand scenario, the increments have been finalized at 18-20% for off-shore associates, higher than earlier anticipated levels. Consequently, we expect the margins for FY'05 to decline by around 100 basis

points from Q2 levels. Our guidance is based on an exchange rate of rupees 45.85 per dollar. The EPS for the quarter is at rupees 5.95, a quarter-on-quarter growth of 8.58%. The receivable days are at 80 as against 78 for the previous quarter. The amount spent on CapEx is rupees 38.70 cores, and we expect CapEx for the financial year to be between 130-150 crores. Net increase in cash and bank balances for the quarter was rupees 32 cores as against rupees 66 crores in the previous quarter. This decrease is primarily because of outflows on account -- dividend of rupees 100 cores and higher CapEx of rupees 20 cores. Under the US GAAP, [inaudible] for the quarter were US $98.9 million and profit for the quarter is at US $37.3 million compared to US $40.9 million under Indian GAAP. The difference of US $3.6 million is primarily because of losses at Nipuna of US $2.7 million on deferred stock compensation charge. The loss of Nipuna includes a non-cash charge of around US $1 million towards redemption premium on preference shares issued by Nipuna. The earnings per ADS for Q2 is 24 cents, which is at last quarter levels. Thank and now we will open the session to Q&A. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, sir. At this moment, I would like to handover the floor to Sarah at CyberBazaar International bridge to conduct the Q&A for participants connected there. After this, we will have a Q&A session at SingTel and then at CyberBazaar India bridge. Thank you and over to Sarah. OPERATOR: Thank you Pratibha. We will now begin the Q&A session for participants outside of India. Please press "*" "1" to ask a question and if you would like to withdraw your question, press the "#" key. We will pause for just a moment to compile the Q&A roster. Your first question comes from Julio Contale (phonetic). HULIO CONTALE, ANALYST: Hi guys, good evening. I guess we're all on the West Coast today. Wanted to start of real quickly with a question about the verticals in particular the

growth in banking and finance versus the decline in manufacturing, can you talk specifically to what's driving, want to accelerate as much as it did and the other to decline; is this related to GE revenues? RAM MYNAMPATI, PRESIDENT, SATYAM COMPUTER SERVICES LTD.: [Hulio] good evening. This is Ram Mynampati here. As far as the increase specific to BSFI is concerned, we have stated in the last few quarters that our endeavor is to broad base our growth, and we continue to focus on those verticals that have been traditional strong course for Satyam. While we did not have the growth rates in line with what we would expected in BSFI earlier, we are happy to report that, you know, it is certainly paying dividend related to our efforts and that we are positive about growth opportunities the BSFI. But I would also like to caution that, you know, quarterly variations as far as distribution of revenues across verticals is bound to happen and I would suggest that we don't read too much into quarterly variations as far as revenue distributions from any other vertical is concerned and you know while there might be a marginal decline as far as contribution from manufacturing is concerned, we certainly don't view that as a trend that we would attribute to a quarterly variation. HULIO CONTALE: And what about the GE revenues in the quarter? Can you talk little bit about how those ramped up? RAM MYNAMPATI: Well, as -- if you know notice, you know, the revenue distribution as far as the top customer is concerned, it has varied in the last few quarters within a fairly narrow band and as we have stated in the previous quarters, you know, while we manage our revenues on an annualized basis there would always be some distribution of revenue across quarters and I would not read too much into that other than to say that we have noticed increase in revenue from the top [next] quarter. HULIO CONTALE: Okay and then finally for me, relative to the U.S. GAAP guidance that you are giving

for fiscal '05, is the 100 basis point decline that Srinivas suggested, is that the same for the U.S. GAAP expectations? V. SRINIVAS: Yes, well, this is Srinivas here. Yes [Hulio] it is -- 100 basis points implies to given U.S. GAAP. HULIO CONTALE: Okay, I got it. Thanks. OPERATOR: Your next question comes from Ashish Thadani. ASHISH THADANI, ANALYST: Yes sir, good morning. Just to pick on the U.S. GAAP guidance of 22-23 cents in the coming quarter that compares with 24 cents in the quarter just gone by, is the decline mainly on account of the salary increments? V. SRINIVAS: Yes, Ashish it is entirely -- are primarily because of that. ASHISH THADANI: Okay and a couple of quick questions. Your offshore revenue seems to be a little flattish compared with other companies where it's rising a little more rapidly, what are the reasons for this and do you anticipate anything different in the next few quarters? RAM MYNAMPATI: Ashish, this is Ram. If you noticed last quarter we actually have reported an increase as far as offshore revenues are concerned; as you would notice in the life cycle of any software projects and software engagement, the initial project starts would more often than what happen at on-site followed by downstream offshore opportunities and as we have stated earlier, it is very difficult to predict how the distribution would pan out in terms of actual revenue realization, either at on-site or offshore. Given that we continue to add customers, given that we continue to have new product starts, you know, there would always be variation as far as distribution of revenue between on-site and offshore. Again, I would not necessarily attribute that to any specific reason or a trend. Our endeavor continues to be to improve our offshore revenue contribution and move as quickly as we can projects to offshore recognizing fairly that that is where the maximum value creation opportunities for our customers would be. ASHISH THADANI: Sure. RAMALINGA RAJU: I would like to add to what Ram has stated, this is Raju here. With an exception

of one of our competitors generally as compared few other larger offshore vendors from India, the proportion of on-site as compared to offshore is in the same vicinity. Our attempt clearly is to be able to enhance the offshore proportion, this is something that we are working towards and are fairly confident that we should be able to bring about the improvements as we go forward. ASHISH THADANI: Great and then one other quick one before I jump off, this would be to do with offshore utilization, which has varied within a very large band over the last few quarters, presumably because of the hiring. Over the next year or two what would be your offshore utilization objective? RAMALINGA RAJU: Well, you know we are -- Ashish, this is Raju here -- we are not in a position to make predictions beyond this financial year. It goes without saying that we would like to have the inflation rates offshore move up as much as possible. Second is, as we look to finding the best possible model, within which we get a lot of global services; it is no longer limited to the on-sites centric services or the offshore centric services that we provide from India. We have found near-shore center to be a fairly compelling -- ready population is some cases, where in customers are looking at a via media, if you will, due to either IT professional constraints or needing to have the services rendered closer to home due to the unique nature of the engagement that we are involved in, so centers [like] to have what we have in those results that just offset of our ongoing Canada the one that we are anticipating setting up in Hungary we are plotting to best category there, this would not only cater to the demands from the markets that these centers cater to but also provide an opportunity for us to take advantage and we are short of expertise. So many of these markets would initially start out as opportunities to trap into the potential that is available in the market but we continue to evaluate whether that center can perhaps be used in more than one way, not just limiting to a business development center, but certainly take advantage of the [inaudible] capabilities available from within those markets. Brazil, we believe that Latin America will certainly be the next market where we believe many substantial opportunities could come from as we continue to pursue the expansion strategy. Our effort is to

set-up an office in Brazil, in Sao Paulo, Brazil in next few weeks and we would use that as an anchor as we pursue our Latin American strategies. But in a while, we were all effected recent initiatives and we may not have a substantial number of employees still to report, if you for instance take our China operations, we have roughly about 100 or so -- in excess of 100 employees working up at our China center, Hungary, we are inaugurating the Hungary office at a short period of time and we certainly are eager to expand our presence in those markets as quickly as we can. MITALI GHOSH: Thanks a lot, just a clarification on this; would you look at this the near-shore centers developing in -- maybe shift of work from on-site to near-shore or more as, you know, for some of the newer customers who may not want to come, you know, for some services who may not want to come offshore, you know, they are moving more to near-shore? V. SRINIVAS: Yeah, I would look at this, Mitali, more from a project or engagement-specific rather than customer-specific. In other words, there are those instances where customers have not looked at services from offshore very favorably due to the unique nature of the engagement, and they did not necessarily at the relationship level, but more at an engagement level. For instance, let's say a customer is building a product that would be fairly core to their business and they cannot afford any compromise on IT or any compromise on security; all of the type of services that we provide in the area of the application that could be seen as mission-critical for the customer, you know, whether it is due to security loss, either due to -- a host of reasons. So, those types of engagements would probably not be available for us if we did not have the near-shore option. So today by taking advantage of the near-shore option, we are able to tap into many more opportunities. So, I would not look at this

necessarily as a substitute for offshore, but I would look at this as an intermediate milestone in the journey towards offshore and thereby providing us a much greater access to opportunities for than otherwise. MITALI GHOSH: Okay, thank very much. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, ma'am. Our next question comes from Mr. Dipen Shah of Kotak Securities. DIPEN SHAH, ANALYST, KOTAK SECURITIES: Congratulation gentlemen on a pretty decent quarter. But all my questions have been answered. Thanks very much. OPERATOR: Thank you very much, sir. At this moment, I would like to hand over the floor back to Mr. Surendra Goyal of SSKI Securities. SURENDRA GOYAL: Yeah, thank you Pratibha. Thanks to the members of Satyam Management, thanks to all the participants, bye and have a nice day. V. SRINIVAS: Thank you. RAMALINGA RAJU: Thank you. OPERATOR: Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for [choosing] CyberBazaar's conferencing services. That concludes this conference call. Thank you for your participation. You may now disconnect.
